data.title: Screen legend Patrick Tse saved my brother from drowning, woman recalls
data.description:
A Hong Kong woman has paid tribute to the late screen legend Patrick Tse Yin, revealing that he saved her brother from drowning decades ago on a boat trip.
Veteran actor, director and producer Tse, nicknamed “Brother Four”, died on July 16 at the age of 89, marking the loss of one of the last remaining icons from the golden era of local cinema, when the city’s film industry held sway across Southeast Asia.
A social media user called “Maykeung46”, the daughter of the late veteran character actor Keung Chung-ping, recalled on Thursday how Tse once saved her younger brother.
She said in a post on Threads that her father had been a former colleague of Tse and that during a Sunday trip on Tse’s boat, her brother began to sink while swimming. Tse immediately jumped into the sea and pulled him to safety, leading her family to regard him as their saviour.


She added that the last time she saw Tse was at her father’s funeral, where the late star offered his condolences and was remembered by her as “forever the most stylish”. Keung died in late December 1999.
Tse’s son, Nicholas Tse Ting-fung, and daughter, Jennifer Tse Ting-ting, announced his death on Monday, sending shock waves through Chinese communities worldwide. Tse’s health had reportedly deteriorated in recent months.
Tenky Tin Kai-man, a spokesman for the Federation of Hong Kong Filmmakers, said Tse died on July 16.

Tse was affectionately known as “Brother Four”, or “Sei Gor” in Cantonese, and was rarely seen without his trademark dark sunglasses.
He earned the nickname because he was the fourth child in his family and also played the character Law Sei-hoi in the gambling-themed drama The Shell Game, with his sidekicks calling him “Brother Four”. The nickname stayed with him.
Tse, alongside actors such as Kenneth Tsang Kong, Chow Chung, Cheung Ying-choi and Bowie Wu Fung – all born in the 1930s – was among the most beloved stars of a then-new wave of Cantonese cinema that resonated with post-war audiences. All but Wu have died in recent years.

data.title: Hong Kong high-speed rail link sets record as passenger trips top 16m in 6 months
data.description:
Hong Kong’s cross-border high-speed rail link carried more than 16 million passengers in the first six months of the year – the highest since its debut in 2018 – prompting the introduction of new facilities to meet demand.
The MTR Corporation, which operates the 26km Guangzhou-Shenzhen-Hong Kong Express Rail Link, said on Wednesday that patronage rose by more than 10 per cent year on year to over 16 million in the first six months, with average daily trips exceeding 92,000.
The company said demand was driven by the growing number of Hongkongers travelling to Shenzhen and beyond for leisure, with passenger flows generally higher on weekends and public holidays, especially during long breaks.
A single-day all-time high of nearly 150,000 trips was recorded on April 4 this year during the Easter and Qing Ming Festival holiday.
Michael Tien Puk-sun, the former chairman of the Kowloon-Canton Railway Corporation, said the future of the MTR Corp would have “a lot to do” with cross-border traffic, noting that domestic routes may not necessarily win business away from buses and other competitors because of affordability.
“So, the cross-boundary [business] is most ideal for the MTR – cross-boundary [passengers] are all new customers,” he said, adding that the company had greater growth potential.

In response to rising demand, the MTR Corp teamed up with Plaza Premium Group to open an upgraded business class lounge at the West Kowloon terminus on Wednesday.
The lounge spans about 8,600 square feet and provides roughly 200 seats, along with charging points for devices, Wi-fi and complimentary refreshments.
The MTR Corp has also streamlined ticket checking, immigration clearance and boarding procedures to 90 minutes before train departure, down from 120 minutes. It has also upgraded seating areas across three levels of the station and enhanced complimentary Wi-fi.
The station now provides direct services to 113 mainland destinations. Additionally, 13 extra pairs of trains will run between Hong Kong West Kowloon and Futian stations on weekends from July 25 to August 23 to meet summer travel demand.

data.title: Hong Kong’s competition watchdog tightens tender clauses to target bid-rigging
data.description:
Hong Kong’s competition watchdog has revised its tender clauses to require bidders make a statutory declaration they have not engaged in collusion on a project, as part of a wider move to criminalise bid-rigging following the city’s deadliest fire in decades.
Senior Counsel Jat Sew-tong, chairman of the Competition Commission, also said on Thursday the body was considering proposing to raise the maximum penalty for the proposed criminal offence from seven to 10 years.
Tendering practices came under the spotlight following last November’s fire at Wang Fuk Court in Tai Po, which killed 168 people and displaced 5,000 residents. At least two bid-rigging syndicates were linked to the tender for the HK$336 million (US$43 million) maintenance project that was under way when the blaze broke out.
Substandard materials used in the renovations were found to have contributed to the rapid spread of the fire.
Unveiled during the commission’s first public general meeting, the revised non-collusion clauses and tendering certificates explicitly state that bidders who sign the documents and engage in collusive conduct could face prosecution for conspiracy to defraud.
The updated documents also introduce a new requirement for bidders to submit a statutory declaration affirming that their bids were prepared independently and free of collusive practices.

First published in 2017 for procurers to incorporate into their invitation-to-bid documents and contracts, the clauses and tendering certificates are currently not compulsory for all building renovation projects, although projects under the Urban Renewal Authority’s “Smart Tender” scheme adopt them.
Jat stressed that under the new regime, bidders who signed the documents could no longer claim a lack of criminal intent, adding that violating a statutory declaration would constitute another criminal offence.
“The bidders at least stated that they had not done anything illegal. This will strengthen confidence,” he said. “For those who want to fish in troubled waters, it will be difficult.”
He called on all procurers to add the non-collusion clauses to their tenders and require bidders to sign them, as a way to protect their own interests.
Ellie Siu Nga-yee, the commission’s senior legal counsel, said the statutory declaration would create a stand-alone offence that would not require proving conspiracy, dishonesty or economic loss in the case, making any criminal prosecution easier.
Siu added that senior management who directed subordinate staff to sign the declarations would not be able to evade liability if they engaged in collusion and are found to instigate or assist someone in making a false statutory statement.
Under current laws, conspiracy to defraud carries a maximum penalty of 14 years’ imprisonment, while making a false statutory declaration carries up to two years in jail.
When asked if the commission would mandate the use of the clauses across all private tenders, Jat pointed to constraints within private business activity.
“It is difficult to enforce the requirements by law, because it involves various industries and occupations,” Jat said.
“In principle, it is private conduct … We hope the model will be widely adopted so it can achieve the same effect.”
Lawyer and former lawmaker Doreen Kong Yuk-foon described the move as positive but questioned its effectiveness, suggesting that a maximum of two years’ imprisonment for making a false statutory declaration would offer limited deterrence against bid-rigging.
Kong stressed that the primary challenge lay in enforcement and proving the existence of agreements between bid-rigging parties.
“It is not about whether they sign the documents or not. Those who collude are not afraid to make that declaration,” Kong said.
“Consider the benefits [from bid-rigging] and compare it with the two years’ imprisonment – doing one or two deals could bring them millions of dollars.”
The commission previously announced plans to establish a “dual-track” framework, comprising both civil and criminal regimes, to criminalise bid-rigging, which is currently only punishable by fines.
While the final report by the independent committee investigating the fire is likely to be delayed until October, Jat indicated that the commission would stick to its original schedule to put forward its proposal as early as September.
On the maximum penalty for the criminal offence of bid-rigging, Jat said the earlier proposed seven years’ imprisonment would only be the minimum and the penalty could be raised to 10 years in jail. He added that the commission remained open to all options.
He also revealed that different ways to set the maximum fine were being considered, suggesting it could be a fixed amount between HK$5 million and HK$15 million or a percentage of the proceeds of the crime.
Established in 2012, the commission is an independent statutory body responsible for investigating anticompetitive conduct and enforcing the law.

data.title: US sanctions on 39 Hong Kong, mainland officials to remain in place: Marco Rubio
data.description:
US Secretary of State Marco Rubio has said that there will be no change to the remaining sanctions on 39 Hong Kong and mainland Chinese officials in the “near future”, while acknowledging the issue came up in a meeting with Foreign Minister Wang Yi.
Rubio, Washington’s top diplomat, also said on Thursday that US President Donald Trump had allowed a national emergency declaration on Hong Kong to expire last week after determining that the “circumstances had changed”, though he did not elaborate further.
Asked by reporters at an Asean foreign ministers’ summit in Manila why Washington had eased sanctions on some officials and allowed the emergency order to expire, Rubio said: “There wasn’t an easing of sanctions.”
He added: “The condition underlying some of those elements no longer existed.”

Trump issued Executive Order 13936 in July 2020, declaring a national emergency after Beijing imposed the national security law on Hong Kong following months of anti-government protests.
The declaration underpinned Washington’s decision to end Hong Kong’s preferential treatment in areas including trade and export controls.
The emergency declaration was allowed to expire last week, although the US State Department quickly clarified that this did not mean Hong Kong’s special status had been restored.
Explaining the reason for allowing the order to expire, Rubio said that Trump felt the “justification and rationale” for it had changed.
“The circumstances had changed in the president’s judgment,” he said, without elaborating.
He added that the matter was discussed “yesterday”, in an apparent reference to his meeting with Wang on Wednesday, during which the pair also discussed a planned trip to the US by President Xi Jinping and the Beijing-Manila dispute in the South China Sea.
“There’s still a significant number of individuals in Hong Kong that remain sanctioned that were not covered by the executive order, and that remains in place,” Rubio said.
“And obviously that was raised with us yesterday, but we don’t see a change any time in the near future on that.”
Under the original executive order, the United States imposed six rounds of sanctions targeting 48 Hong Kong and mainland officials between 2020 and 2025.


Following the expiry of the declaration, sanctions were removed on nine Hong Kong and mainland officials, including Secretary for Justice Paul Lam Ting-kwok, Dong Jingwei, director of Beijing’s Office for Safeguarding National Security in Hong Kong, and two former police commissioners, Raymond Siu Chak-yee and Stephen Lo Wai-chung.
The other 39 officials, including Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu, his predecessor Carrie Lam Cheng Yuet-ngor and Secretary for Security Chris Tang Ping-keung, continue to face sanctions under the Hong Kong Autonomy Act, legislation enacted in 2020 after the national security law came into force.
Both China’s Ministry of Commerce and the Hong Kong government described Washington’s move last week as an “important step” towards implementing a consensus reached between Beijing and Washington.
The US, when introducing the executive order, said the security law had eroded Hong Kong’s autonomy and restricted rights and freedoms promised to the city after its return to Chinese rule in 1997. Beijing and Hong Kong authorities have maintained that the legislation restored stability after the 2019 protests.

data.title: Herzog & de Meuron makes second donation to Hong Kong’s M+ museum
data.description:
International architecture firm Herzog & de Meuron, which built Hong Kong’s M+ museum and the National Stadium in Beijing, has donated a second collection of models, sketches and drawings to the art institution in the West Kowloon Cultural District.
The M+ said on Thursday that the more than 200 archival items follow an initial donation in 2023, when the architecture firm, headquartered in Switzerland, gave the museum a section model of the National Stadium.
In addition to designing the stadium, commonly known as the “Bird’s Nest”, for the 2008 Beijing Olympics, the firm was responsible for the striking upside down T-shaped M+ building and the Tai Kwun heritage centre, as well as the Tate Modern in London.

“As M+ marks its fifth anniversary this year, this second phase of donation from Herzog & de Meuron further strengthens the M+ collections and affirms architecture as a core pillar of the museum,” museum director Suhanya Raffel said.
“From the design of our museum building as a global cultural landmark to its contribution to our collection, our close relationship with Herzog & de Meuron continues to flourish through this exhibition. It illuminates how architectural design forges connections between people and place, with M+ standing as a powerful testament to that vision.”
An accompanying exhibition, “Herzog & de Meuron: In Focus”, that traces the architecture firm’s work in China through 10 projects between 2002 and 2021 is set to open on September 12.
The exhibition will feature models, drawings, material samples and prototypes donated to the M+, which will be displayed in replicas of the wooden vitrines developed for the firm’s open storage of its archival holdings in Basel.

data.title: US eyes ban on Chinese humanoid robots as US-China tech rivalry intensifies
data.description:
US scrutiny of Chinese technology has expanded to a new frontier, as lawmakers in Washington advance defence legislation prohibiting the military from deploying Chinese-made humanoid robots.
The US House of Representatives has passed the National Defence Authorisation Act (NDAA), an annual military policy bill that incorporates provisions restricting the use of foreign autonomous systems over national security and data privacy concerns.
Under Section 163 of the draft bill, the Pentagon would be barred from procuring, leasing or operating covered humanoid robotic systems linked to designated “foreign adversaries”, including China, Russia and Iran.
While the defence bill incorporates key elements of the proposed Guarding the US Against Adversarial Robotics Dominance (GUARD) Act – introduced last summer by Representative John Moolenaar, chairman of a House select committee focusing on competition with China – it excludes the legislative proposal’s most sweeping measures.
Under the full GUARD Act framework, Chinese humanoid and quadruped “robot dog” systems would be evaluated for inclusion on the Federal Communications Commission’s “Covered List”. That designation would strip the machines of their wireless communication licences, effectively banning their commercial sale across the US market.
By limiting Section 163 strictly to military procurement, lawmakers have left commercial sales untouched for now.
Following the House vote, Moolenaar welcomed the military restrictions but warned that Washington “cannot allow these robots to become predominant in our country”.
His call underscores China’s growing sway in the global robotics market.
Last year, Chinese companies shipped about 80 per cent of the world’s humanoid robots, according to a report by Counterpoint Research in January.
Chinese developer Unitree Robotics said earlier this year that it shipped more than 5,500 humanoid robots in 2025, while rival AgiBot shipped 5,168 units, according to Omdia data published in January.
By comparison, leading American developers – including Figure AI, Agility Robotics and Tesla – each shipped roughly 150 units last year.

data.title: China’s tech firms push beyond hardware to power the global AI boom
data.description:
Driven by a global surge in AI spending and fierce competition at home, Chinese tech companies are rapidly expanding their international footprint, not just via physical supply chains, but increasingly through cloud-based software and foundation models.
The broader push aligns with recent customs data from the first half of 2026 showing a sharp rise in physical tech exports. Integrated-circuit exports nearly doubled in value to US$177.3 billion, a 96 per cent year-on-year surge heavily inflated by global chip price hikes.
Meanwhile, exports of automatic data-processing equipment jumped over 41 per cent to US$138.1 billion, and industrial-robot shipments climbed over 18 per cent to US$929 million across 141 countries and regions.
However, corporate disclosures reveal a far broader dynamic beyond physical goods: mainland Chinese firms are carving out key positions across both the software and hardware layers of the global AI boom.
Shanghai-based AI lab MiniMax generated US$57.7 million, or 73 per cent of its total revenue, outside mainland China in 2025 – up from US$21.3 million a year earlier, according to its financial statements.
By the end of 2025, it had cumulatively served more than 214,000 enterprise customers and developers from over 100 countries and regions. Its flagship M2 model became the first Chinese model on OpenRouter to cross 50 billion in daily token consumption, the company said.
Chinese AI developers were leveraging lower development costs and competitive product pricing to scale globally, as their models approached performance levels capable of driving broader international adoption, Goldman Sachs said in a recent report.
However, commercial strategies among Chinese players vary sharply based on their target markets.
Enterprise-focused model developer Zhipu AI, for example, remains deeply anchored at home. Its 2025 revenue was “substantially derived” from customers in China, according to its annual report. On-premise deployments, which require installing AI software onto a client’s internal servers, generated 534 million yuan (US$78.9 million), or nearly 74 per cent of total sales.

The divide underscores a growing reality in Chinese software: lightweight consumer apps and developer-facing cloud services can easily go global, while heavy enterprise deployments require localised implementation and support networks.
Chinese optical-module makers have become indispensable to global tech giants building massive computing clusters amid a global AI infrastructure boom. Their products carry data between servers, switches and computing nodes within data centres, making them increasingly important as cloud providers expand AI computing and networking capacity.
Following the exit of most US and Japanese competitors from the optical-transceiver market by 2020, Chinese vendors stepped in to fill the void, according to research firm LightCounting.
Zhongji Innolight has emerged as the leading global supplier to major cloud providers, the research firm said. Its Hong Kong listing prospectus showed that the US market alone generated over 57 per cent of its revenue last year.
Meanwhile, Eoptolink jumped to second place globally, according to LightCounting, as it established itself as a key supplier of 400-gigabit and 800-gigabit components to Amazon while also securing product qualification with Nvidia and other major US tech firms.
Both Chinese companies generated over 90 per cent of their 2025 revenue overseas, directly reaping the rewards of massive AI capital expenditure by Western tech giants.
For Chinese robotics vendors, the overseas push is driven as much by local survival as it is by global opportunity. Pushed outward by intense competition at home, mobile-robot vendors with international operations are significantly outperforming their domestic-only peers, according to Interact Analysis.
“The saturated and highly competitive Chinese market is forcing leading players to look outward,” the research firm said.
The firm pointed to a stark price arbitrage, estimating that an autonomous forklift selling for US$15,000 to US$20,000 in China can command US$40,000 to US$60,000 in Europe and North America, leaving comfortable profit margins even after localisation and support costs.
Beijing-based warehouse-automation company Geekplus illustrates the financial power of this strategy.
Its revenue from outside mainland China reached 2.39 billion yuan in 2025, accounting for over 75 per cent of its total, while almost 80 per cent of new orders came from overseas markets – led by a growth rate of more than 50 per cent in the Americas.

data.title: Zhongji sets highest subscription threshold in Hong Kong IPO history
data.description:
Zhongji Innolight, the largest IPO in Hong Kong in nearly seven years, has set the highest subscription threshold in the city’s history, dampening retail investors’ appetite, though institutional demand remains strong, with multiple oversubscriptions already placed, according to people familiar with the matter.
Priced at a maximum of HK$1,010 per share and requiring 50 shares per board lot, the minimum threshold is HK$51,009.29 (US$6505.49), making it the most expensive initial public offering entry threshold in Hong Kong’s history.
The company began its Hong Kong public offering on Wednesday, offering 54.5 million H-shares globally, with 10 per cent allocated to retail investors.
As the city’s largest IPO since 2019, Zhongji had successfully attracted substantial institutional interest, with its international placing already oversubscribed, according to sources.
Stocks are traded in so-called “lots” in the city, with the Hong Kong Exchanges & Clearing recently standardising board lot sizes into eight tiers – 1, 50, 100, 500, 1,000, 2,000, 5,000, and 10,000 shares – in order to lower barriers for retail investors, reduce odd lots and boost market liquidity.
High entry thresholds are also seen in nearby markets. In Taiwan, Hon. Precision, an integrated circuit test handler solutions provider, set a record in December with an issue price of NT$1,495, requiring NT$1.495 million per lot.
Meanwhile, Zhongji A-shares require 100 shares per lot, exceeding 100,000 yuan (US$14,769.11) at current market prices.

In contrast, US stock markets do not enforce board lot minimums, allowing investors to buy single shares. However, US IPO allocations follow a pro-rata rule based on individual subscription amounts relative to total market demand.
Alvin Poon, an associate director at Prudential Brokerage, a local brokerage specialising in IPO margin financing, highlighted three main factors that were dampening retail appetite: high entry costs discouraging short-term traders, a narrow A-share price discount of less than 20 per cent, and recent cooling in semiconductor-related stocks and overall IPO sentiment.
Margin financing data from major brokerages showed retail subscription funds exceeded HK$22 billion (US$2.8 billion) by the second day, representing an oversubscription of more than three times that of the retail tranche target.
Zhongji has secured 33 cornerstone investors, including Temasek, Abu Dhabi Investment Authority (ADIA), Alibaba Group Holding, which owns the South China Morning Post, and Tencent Holdings. They have committed over HK$27 billion, accounting for almost half of the total IPO fundraising proceeds.
The public offering will close on Monday, with official trading scheduled to begin on July 30. Goldman Sachs, China International Capital Corporation, Morgan Stanley and GF Securities are serving as joint sponsors.

data.title: Son of ex-Chinese official jailed for 6 years, 9 months for deception, laundering
data.description:
The son of a former mainland Chinese official has been jailed for six years and nine months for using deceptive means to secure residency in Hong Kong and laundering more than HK$64 million (US$8.2 million), including a bribe his father allegedly sought a decade ago.
The District Court on Thursday sentenced Xiao Rui, 37, on four counts of money laundering and one of using false instruments for offences he committed between 2013 and 2023.
Deputy Judge Bernard Chung Wai-keung said Xiao had undermined the effectiveness of a cash-for-residency scheme by deceiving the Immigration Department into granting him a two-year visa using two bogus bank statements.
While acknowledging the lack of evidence showing Xiao knew the origin of the illegal proceeds, Chung said the defendant had nonetheless handled the ill-gotten gains himself by channelling them to the city through an underground bank over nine years.
Xiao’s father, Xiao Jun, is a former anti-corruption chief at the People’s Procuratorate in Wuhan, while his mother worked as a hospital clerk in the mainland city. The couple is currently under investigation by mainland authorities.
The court heard that the younger Xiao applied for residency in Hong Kong through the old Capital Investment Entrant Scheme in 2013, as a Gambian national.
Under the policy, mainland citizens needed to be a permanent resident in another foreign country to be eligible. About 90 per cent of all approved cases were Chinese nationals with permanent residence abroad, according to official data.
In early 2013, Xiao submitted to immigration authorities two forged bank documents claiming he had 10 million yuan at China Construction Bank.
Despite receiving his visa the following year, Xiao did not move to Hong Kong, but remained in Wuhan before relocating to Shenzhen with his wife in 2019.
Xiao also handled more than HK$64 million in illegal gains in Hong Kong between March 2014 and November 2023.
They included four sums totalling HK$4.72 million from a subsidiary of a Hubei-based industrial investment firm in 2016.

The firm’s chairman, Yao Qian, said they were illicit payments made at the elder Xiao’s request after the latter helped him secure a 50 million yuan water pump project commissioned by the Wuhan government over 10 years ago.
Xiao denied any knowledge of the bribery scheme and insisted the HK$64 million was either investment money given by his mother or proceeds from cryptocurrency sales.
He claimed his mother had made a fortune through investing in a gardening design company and an electrical engineering business run by his uncle.
The court rejected the defendant’s assertions as far-fetched and illogical, while finding the money Xiao had handled far exceeded what he could have earned through lawful means.
The defendant’s lawyer, Edwin Choy Wai-bond SC, said in mitigation his client had accumulated an eight-digit loss since arriving in Hong Kong to invest in asset management, securities and properties.
Choy urged the court to show leniency to Xiao, who would be separated from his wife and six-year-old daughter for a prolonged period after sentencing.

data.title: China, long seen as the world’s factory, is now building AI ‘token factories’The surge in compute capacity underpins Beijing’s ambition to establish itself as a national ‘AI highland’.
data.description:
Beijing is ramping up its computing power and unveiling new policies to boost the city’s “token economy”, as China accelerates AI adoption across key industries in its tech race with the US.
The Chinese capital aims to add another 50,000 petaflops of intelligent computing power in the second half of 2026, pushing its total capacity over 130,000 petaflops by the end of the year, the Beijing Municipal Bureau of Economy and Information Technology said at a press briefing on Tuesday.
One petaflop is equal to 1 trillion calculations per second.
The aggressive target follows a 22,000-petaflop boost in the first six months of the year, which lifted the city’s current supply to 82,000 petaflops, according to data from the local technology agency.
The surge in compute capacity underpins Beijing’s ambition to establish itself as a national “AI highland”, supporting the country’s broader push to integrate artificial intelligence into traditional industries to spark new economic growth.
Beijing also aims to foster an open-source “RISC-V + AI OS” ecosystem.
By pairing AI-focused operating systems with RISC-V, an open-standard chip architecture seen as a key alternative to proprietary Western tech like Arm and Intel’s x86, the city hopes to build a full-stack framework spanning chips and applications.

Alongside the hardware push, Beijing pledged to formulate targeted policies for the token economy – governing the production, distribution and application of the basic units used to measure and bill AI service usage.
To support this, the city planned to construct “token factories” – next-generation AI data centres designed to maximise token output through efficient computing and energy use. The agency said these facilities would drive real-world applications across key sectors, including manufacturing, education and tourism.
The municipal road map reflects a nationwide effort to expand China’s computing power grid to keep pace with skyrocketing demand for AI tokens. Daily token calls across China surpassed 140 trillion in March, a more than 1,000-fold increase compared to early 2024, according to official figures.
Beijing Yihao, the capital’s first token factory which was completed last month, said it had already achieved a daily production capacity exceeding 1.4 trillion tokens, alongside a growing wave of similar projects taking root across the country.
As Beijing accelerates its domestic digital economy, Chinese officials are also calling for deeper international collaboration on AI and digital infrastructure, particularly among Global South and Belt and Road Initiative partner nations.
Such technological cooperation would be “an important source of growth momentum in the digital, connected and intelligent era,” said Ren Xianliang, secretary general of the World Internet Conference, at the Digital Silk Road Development Forum in Xian on Wednesday.
The World Internet Conference is a Beijing-based agency promoting China’s cyberspace governance model and vision.
At the same event, Zhuang Rongwen, head of China’s cyberspace regulator, highlighted the growth opportunities available to BRI economies through joint digital connectivity projects, while calling for accelerated digital transformation across traditional sectors.

data.title: Hong Kong Disneyland rolls out afternoon tickets, extra cooling to beat the heat
data.description:
Hong Kong Disneyland Resort has introduced a discounted afternoon ticket and expanded its cooling facilities to help minimise operational disruptions arising from hot weather and heavy rain over the summer.
The theme park on Lantau Island on Thursday unveiled a “Summer Chill” 4pm entry ticket for Hong Kong residents, priced at HK$480 (US$61), allowing guests to enter later in the day to avoid the hottest hours or periods of heavy rain.
The park said it had also stepped up weather adaptation measures, including installing about 860 fans, portable air conditioning units, mist cooling systems and additional shading blinds.
The resort also upgraded nearly half of its more than 40 free water dispensers to improve flow rates, a representative said.
The park added that it would closely monitor the weather and adjust outdoor show schedules when the government’s heat stress at work warnings are in effect.
Frontline staff will also get extra rest breaks, hydration support and access to cooling facilities during periods of extreme heat and humidity.

The measures come after typhoons and bad weather contributed to a 36 per cent year-on-year contraction in net profit to HK$536 million for the financial year ending September 30 last year. Still, that profit was the second-highest in the park’s 20-year history.
During that fiscal year, 15 typhoons, including several super typhoons, and five black rainstorms forced temporary closures, prompting Disneyland to consider adding more covered and indoor experiences.
The latest initiatives encourage visitors to utilise indoor attractions and entertainment during the hottest parts of the day.
The park said staff had analysed sunlight, temperature, humidity and wind, alongside computer simulations, to guide the design of facilities, including shaded seating areas and improved airflow in outdoor spaces.
Disneyland said guests visiting during its ongoing Pixar Summer Fest could also take part in a water-themed parade and enjoy a range of themed frozen treats designed for the summer season.

data.title: Hong Kong checks ‘almost every book shipment  from Taiwan’
data.description:
Taiwan’s Mainland Affairs Council (MAC) has said Hong Kong customs has stepped up scrutiny of “almost every book shipment” from the island, while a logistics company has separately told bookstores to ensure their orders will not breach the city’s national security laws.
Independent bookstores and logistics companies also confirmed on Wednesday that customs had stepped up checks on book shipments from Taiwan in recent months.
In a reply to the South China Morning Post, a spokesman for the Customs and Excise Department said it was fully committed to safeguarding national security and would seize any seditious items discovered and refer cases to police.
The MAC told the SCMP the increase in inspections followed amendments in March to the implementation rules of Hong Kong’s national security law, which empower authorities to seize items deemed seditious.
“Since March and April this year, almost all orders of books from Taiwan have been checked by Hong Kong customs; compared with once every several months previously, the checks are more frequent,” an MAC spokesman said.
The spokesman expressed concern that Hong Kong authorities were continually curtailing the freedom of speech and publication, saying Taiwan would always welcome Hongkongers to “read and purchase books”.
The MAC’s remarks followed a notice issued by Jaguar Logistics, a Hong Kong-based firm with branches in mainland China and Taiwan, urging local bookstores to ensure their shipments did not breach national security legislation.
The company stated it would not ship books that violated the law, as well as orders from “bookstores under authorities’ investigation”.
The notice came at a time of rising pressure on independent bookstores in Hong Kong.
Leticia Wong Man-huen and her husband, the owners of Hunter’s Bookstore in Sham Shui Po, were arrested last month on suspicion of sedition and money laundering.

Last week, police raided Have a Nice Stay bookshop in Prince Edward and Greenfield Book Store in Mong Kok. Officers seized books and arrested five people for allegedly displaying and selling seditious publications.
All those arrested have been released on bail.
Confirming the notice, a staff member from Jaguar’s Taiwan branch told the SCMP that Hong Kong authorities had been inspecting books from the island more frequently, but said this was presented to them as normal procedure.
He said that submitting a shipment list had always been a standard export requirement, though the company would now scrutinise shipments more closely to determine whether they might breach the law.
“Whether a book violates the national security law is determined by the Hong Kong government, but some books that obviously [violate the law], for example Let Only Red Flowers Bloom, we will not ship,” the staff said.
Let Only Red Flowers Bloom: Identity and Belonging in Xi Jinping’s China, written by American journalist Emily Feng about people who pushed back against Chinese rule, was reportedly the publication deemed seditious that triggered police raids and arrests at Have a Nice Stay and Greenfield Book Store last week.
The staff member also confirmed that Jaguar would stop shipments to the bookshops under investigation.
“For example, Have a Nice Stay was our customer, but we told them we will not be able to serve them any more, because the incident had caused a scene,” the staff member said.
Several independent bookstore owners, speaking on condition of anonymity, said customs scrutiny on recent shipments had become more rigorous
“Our shipments were rarely inspected in the past; however, around 70 per cent of our shipments were checked in recent months,” an owner said.
Owners also said Jaguar had been a primary courier for the industry due to its partnership with a major Taiwan book distributor, efficient services and long-standing cooperation.
They estimated that Taiwan titles accounted for 60 to 70 per cent of the inventory in local independent bookstores, warning that failing to seek alternative logistics channels could severely cripple their businesses.
Secretary for Security Chris Tang Ping-keung previously said that booksellers were responsible for ensuring the titles they sold did not violate national security legislation, and reiterated that the authorities would not compile a list of “banned books”.

data.title: Alibaba unit says 5-in-1 AI gives robots unified brain, body
data.description:
Alibaba Group Holding’s mapping unit has unveiled an upgrade to what it calls the world’s first technology framework that unites a robot’s “feet, hands, brains, central nerves and motor nerves” into a single system.
Amap’s ABot system marks the latest move in Chinese tech players’ race to harness artificial intelligence models to make robots more capable. Referred to as embodied intelligence, the effort aims to equip machines with the “brains” and other elements required to navigate and complete tasks in complex physical spaces.
With five new specialised foundation AI models working together, ABot aimed to address a systemic problem in the industry, according to Amap: the lack of a complete, self-evolving system.
“The industry has long focused on breakthroughs in single models – better navigation models, more powerful operation models and smarter reasoning models – but these models operate independently without shared data and experience,” it said in a social media post on Wednesday.
With its five models – ABot-N1, ABot-M0.5, ABot-ER, ABot-AgentOS, and ABot-C0 – working in concert, the Amap system sought to shatter those silos, according to the firm.
The ABot-N1 model employed an “eyes guiding feet” approach, enabling a robot to handle city-scale autonomous navigation with a 92.9 per cent outdoor success rate using only standard cameras and basic road networks, Amap said on Wednesday, adding that this bypassed the need for costly high-definition maps.
Meanwhile, ABot-M0.5 allowed a machine to “use hands and feet simultaneously”, divorcing movement functions from manipulation tasks so a robot could seamlessly grasp objects while walking, the company said.
ABot-ER and ABot-AgentOS acted as brain and central nervous system, respectively, helping to link tasks to objects, and then adapting across heterogeneous robotic bodies, according to Amap. ABot-C0 served as the “motor nerves” that finally translated the decisions of the ABot system into robot actions, it said.

The framework had secured state-of-the-art results across 17 global robotics benchmarks, the company added.
Tencent Holdings launched its own full-stack embodied intelligence offering at the latest World AI Conference in Shanghai last week, debuting its Hy-Embodied vision-language-action models to help robots perceive and reason.
Robbyant, the embodied AI arm of Chinese fintech giant Ant Group, earlier this month launched LingBot-Depth 2.0, its next-generation spatial perception model, which was designed to help robots accurately perceive glass, mirrors and transparent objects.
Ant is the fintech affiliate of Alibaba Group Holding, which also owns the South China Morning Post.
Amap, an increasingly important player in Alibaba’s AI push, has been pushing deeper into embodied intelligence in recent months. It also stepped up research into so-called world models – systems designed to simulate real-world environments to train robots to understand the physical world.

data.title: University to roll out screenings at pharmacies to uncover osteoporosis cases
data.description:
The University of Hong Kong will roll out a screening programme at pharmacies across the city early next year to identify people at high risk of osteoporosis and prevent bone fractures.
The institution’s Li Ka Shing Faculty of Medicine on Thursday launched the city’s first community pharmacist-led osteoporosis screening programme.
The scheme uses a screening algorithm that allows pharmacists to identify high-risk patients during routine visits and refer them to family doctors for bone density scans and diagnosis.
Osteoporosis affects about 30 per cent of women and 8 per cent of men aged 50 and older in Hong Kong. But up to 80 per cent of cases go undiagnosed and untreated because the disease is asymptomatic until a fracture occurs.
Fewer than one in 10 at-risk individuals proactively seek bone density screening.
The one-year mortality rate following a hip fracture exceeds that of prostate and breast cancer, while local hospital data shows that nearly half of hip fracture patients do not survive beyond five years.
“The high death toll is driven less by the fracture itself than by complications from prolonged immobility and bed rest, chiefly pneumonia and other infections that claim elderly patients in the months after injury,” said Dr Cheung Ching-lung, an associate professor at the university’s pharmacology and pharmacy department.
The Chinese Osteoporosis Screening Algorithm uses four questions covering age, weight, sex and fracture history to assess risk, achieving a reported accuracy rate of 74 to 94 per cent.
Under a pilot programme that ran from January to May, four community pharmacies approached 1,082 people deemed “high-risk individuals”, who are defined as men aged 70 or above, women aged 65 and over or adults aged 50 or above with at least one additional risk factor.

Among the 643 people who agreed to take part, 18.2 per cent were identified as being at very high risk and referred to family physicians for further evaluation.
One of the four pilot sites was the Diamond Hill Family Pharmacy, run by the Christian Family Service Centre, where an 84-year-old woman surnamed Shum was invited to undergo a screening while she collected a blood glucose meter.
“I did not expect I would develop osteoporosis, but I learned the consequences can be serious, and it is good to prevent it,” she said.
Flagged as high risk, she was referred to a family doctor and given personalised advice on exercise and calcium intake.
The university has also launched an online continuing pharmacy education course to instruct pharmacists on standardised guidelines and referral pathways.
By the start of next year, the service will be rolled out to community pharmacies across the city, including NGO-run and private outlets, before expanding into district health centres and other primary care platforms as part of a comprehensive bone health network.

data.title: Hong Kong officers lose appeal over framing homeless man, evidence cover-up
data.description:
Four disgraced Hong Kong police officers have lost their appeal against convictions and jail terms for framing a homeless man for drug possession and destroying evidence of the cover-up six years ago.
In a written judgment on Thursday, the Court of Appeal said the officers must have intended to pervert the course of justice when they tried to obstruct surveillance cameras while colleagues fabricated evidence against a street sleeper during an anti-drug operation on February 4, 2020.
The three presiding judges rejected the officers’ claims that they did not intend to prejudice subsequent legal proceedings and that tampering with surveillance footage had no material impact on the prosecution.
“Common sense tells us that merging evidence collected at two locations, covering it with blankets and placing it in a bag while acting as if nothing had happened is inherently absurd,” Mr Justice Derek Pang Wai-cheong said on behalf of the panel.
“This course of conduct alone provides a sufficient basis to infer that officers were fabricating evidence, and that attempts to obstruct cameras were aimed at avoiding any record of the misconduct.”
The court also declined to reduce the four officers’ sentences, citing the seriousness of the offences and their adverse impact on the administration of justice.
Constables Leung Fei-pang, Pong Chun-sze, Chan Sau-yip and Wan Pak-sze were among six officers convicted of five counts of perverting the course of justice over the incident at Tung Chau Street Park in Sham Shui Po.

Leung, Pong and Chan obscured the view of two CCTV cameras while colleagues framed Le Van Muoi by placing narcotics seized from other homeless people into a black cylinder that police falsely claimed belonged to him.
Wan also made false entries in her notebook and witness statement, claiming the suspected drugs had been seized from Le’s tent.
In October 2024, the District Court sentenced Chan and Wan to three years and one month in prison, while Leung and Pong received two years and one month.
Police sergeant Lam Wah-ka and constable Mok Chi-shing, who were jailed for three years and five months and three years and one month respectively, did not appeal.
Constables Kwok Chin-sing and Hon Ting-kwong were acquitted of charges related to the alleged assault of street sleeper Nguyen Van Son in the same trial, after the court found him to be an unreliable witness.
Le, who had multiple prior drug convictions, died in October 2020 after he was found to have strangled himself while on remand in a psychiatric facility.

data.title: RedNote’s AI model is first to achieve flawless score at maths Olympiad
data.description:
For the first time, an artificial intelligence model has achieved a perfect score at the International Mathematical Olympiad (IMO) – and it was developed by Chinese social giant RedNote.
The model, dots-note-3.0, solved all six puzzles and secured a perfect score of 42 at this year’s IMO, which concluded in Shanghai on Monday.
The note variant, still in beta phase, is currently the lightest model in the dots3 family, which includes two larger versions – jazz and aria – tailored for different use cases and compute costs.
Shanghai-based RedNote, known for its Instagram-style social community called Xiaohongshu in Chinese, said it would open-source dots-note-3.0 “in the near future”, without providing further details, according to a post on the model’s website.
Held annually since 1959, the IMO is widely regarded as the world’s most prestigious mathematics competition, including for AI systems. Unlike answer-only exams where only the final results need to be judged, IMO problem-solving requires proof backed up by rigorous arguments, without logical errors or missing conditions.
Earlier attempts by AI have scored fairly well. At last year’s IMO, Google DeepMind and OpenAI achieved gold-medal-level performances, both earning 35 out of 42 points.

At IMO 2024, Google DeepMind’s AlphaProof and AlphaGeometry 2 scored 28, a silver-medal equivalent, but required human experts to manually translate problem statements into logic language for the AI to process them.
This year, RedNote processed the original maths competition documents directly, without the need for humans to translate them into specialised coding languages. The system operated end-to-end under official contest rules that strictly barred any human intervention, such as providing hints, correcting reasoning steps or completing logic.
Dots-note-3.0 relied on an “agentic” framework that combined natural-language reasoning with Python programming code execution to draft, test and refine its proofs.
According to RedNote, the system’s strong performance was rooted in its ability to scrutinise its own reasoning, question assumptions and continuously fix flawed logic through a multi-step self-review loop before submitting final solutions.
Separately, the Chinese human team consisting of six members secured first place at the IMO, with three achieving a full score.

data.title: Chinese bank touts digital currency platform’s largest transfers yet
data.description:
A leading Chinese bank has processed the largest cross-border transactions yet via mBridge, a multilateral central bank digital currency (CBDC) platform, marking a major milestone for the emerging payment network as well as Beijing’s global yuan ambitions.
Two transactions, worth more than US$1.7 billion each, were carried out by the Bank of China’s Shenzhen and Fujian branches in June and July, respectively. They showed mBridge’s ability to support high-value cross-border settlements across different currencies and business scenarios, the lender said on Tuesday.
In June, an outbound transfer of 11.3 billion yuan (US$1.7 billion) delivered same-day, full-value funds to a corporate client via the network – which comprises central banks from mainland China, Hong Kong, Macau, Saudi Arabia, United Arab Emirates (UAE) and Thailand.
The following month, an inbound transaction in Hong Kong dollars moved from an overseas sender to an onshore corporate account in under 60 minutes. The bank said this transaction set the record for the largest transaction on the platform since its inception, but did not specify the amount.
The landmark transfers signal China’s broader strategic effort to construct alternative cross-border clearing rails, accelerating the internationalisation of the Chinese currency while bypassing traditional, Western-centric financial networks.
The mBridge project seeks to reshape cross-border payments by using blockchain technology to enable faster settlement through CBDCs. Unlike traditional international payment channels, which rely on chains of intermediary banks, incurring high fees and settlement delays, mBridge enables direct, peer-to-peer settlement.
As an early participant in the platform’s pilot programmes, the Bank of China has increased its mBridge activity since commercialising the service in April 2024.
In June, the bank processed Macau’s first mBridge transaction since the city joined the platform, an e-CNY transfer to Hong Kong exceeding 20 million yuan.
On the same day, Bank of China’s Macau branch executed the city’s first mBridge payment to the UAE, a deal that “broadened currency circulation channels towards the Middle East and unlocked the cross-border value of digital finance”, according to domestic media reports.
By late June, total cumulative transactions on mBridge passed 600 billion yuan, with the lender maintaining a dominant lead in volume and enterprise deployment, according to the lender’s data.
Beijing’s effort to construct alternative cross-border clearing infrastructure comes as global yuan adoption faces ongoing headwinds.
The currency accounted for just under 2 per cent of global foreign exchange reserves in the first quarter of 2026, data from the International Monetary Fund showed.
Meanwhile, the yuan’s share of global payments processed by Brussels-based Swift, the predominant interbank network, dropped to 2.75 per cent in May, maintaining the currency’s sixth-place ranking worldwide.
Analysts said mBridge was a critical mechanism for Beijing to mitigate geopolitical risks and reduce reliance on traditional dollar-dominated rails.
The project was “envisioned as a means to break away from this status quo and secure a mechanism for [yuan]-denominated settlements, even in environments with heightened geopolitical risks and active economic sanctions”, said a July report published by the Japan Research Institute.
Gary Ng, senior economist at Natixis Corporate and Investment Bank, said the platform’s expanding range of transactions would be critical to its long-term success.
“The more mBridge use cases there are, the higher the platform’s maturity,” he said, adding that such evolution could help position the platform as an alternative channel alongside Swift while supporting the internationalisation of the yuan.
He noted that although transactions were still being conducted on a case-by-case basis, their growing size suggested significant potential for wider adoption.
Looking ahead, Bank of China said in a statement that it planned to further expand its mBridge offerings by upgrading cross-border payment infrastructure, supporting additional currencies and broadening its geographic reach to both assist corporate clients operating globally and promote internationalisation of the yuan.

data.title: Hong Kong gears up for city’s first test run of fully driverless vehicles
data.description:
Hong Kong has approved a trial scheme for fully driverless autonomous vehicles, with the technology to be tested on Airport Island without a person at the wheel as a backup for the first time.
The Transport Department announced on Thursday that it had expanded the scope of a pilot licence for autonomous vehicle trial schemes in North Lantau, after concluding that earlier remote-operation tests had met safety requirements.
“We are pleased to confirm that the autonomous vehicle tests have been ideal, safe and operating smoothly,” Commissioner for Transport Winnie Tse Wing-yee said.
Since February, autonomous vehicle trials on Airport Island were required to have a person inside the car. A second operator would also monitor the journey remotely from a control centre.
The latest arrangement allows for humans to be removed from the car entirely, while the car will still be monitored remotely.
Chinese technology giant Baidu received Hong Kong’s first pilot licence for autonomous vehicles in November 2024.
Since then, the company has gradually expanded its testing programme from Airport Island to North Lantau, Kowloon East and Southern district, while increasing the number of vehicles, raising speed limits and conducting designated passenger test runs.
Tse said operators would be required to continue submitting monthly reports on the trial runs, adding that the department would carry out on-site inspections and review operations at the remote control centre.
She also said that safety would “always be [the government’s] top consideration” and noted operators were required to formulate detailed safety management and contingency plans covering vehicle, road and cybersecurity risks.
Each test vehicle must also carry valid third-party insurance and be equipped with an electronic data recorder, while new labels stating that tests were in progress will need to be displayed to help other road users identify the vehicles.
Asked whether Hong Kong would need to amend its laws before Level 4 autonomous vehicles could operate on public roads without on-board attendants, Tse said the city’s existing legal framework was already capable of regulating the latest phase of testing.
“Our legislative framework has already provided a very flexible and robust framework to regulate and facilitate the development of autonomous vehicles in Hong Kong,” she said.

Level 4 autonomous vehicles can self-drive without human intervention within designated operating areas and conditions.
The classification is part of the globally recognised six-level automation standard developed by United States-based engineering standards body SAE International, which ranges from Level 0, with no driving automation, to Level 5, where vehicles are capable of fully autonomous operation under all conditions.
Tse added that authorities were processing applications for commercial autonomous vehicle operations and larger vehicle types, while any expansion of testing into new districts would depend on operators’ business plans.
Tse declined to say when driverless testing would progress to the next stage, only saying there was no fixed timetable.
She said the department would continue assessing operators’ monthly reports and conducting inspections, adding that meeting prescribed testing mileage alone would not automatically qualify a project to advance.
Baidu, whose Apollo Go platform is conducting the Airport Island trial scheme, welcomed the approval and described it as “an important milestone” for the development of autonomous driving in Hong Kong.
Yang Nan, vice-president of Baidu and general manager of its overseas intelligent driving business, said the company would launch what it described as the world’s first driverless trial scheme in a right-hand-drive market later this month.
He said the company had spent more than a year testing in Hong Kong after receiving the licence in 2024.
By the end of May, Apollo Go vehicles had travelled more than 240,000km (149,000 miles) in Hong Kong, Yang said.
“We will continue to develop our autonomous driving business in Hong Kong in a safe and effective manner, accelerate unmanned and large-scale operations, and strive to achieve commercial operations as soon as possible,” he said.
The government has repeatedly said it wants autonomous driving technology to progress towards “driverless, scaled-up and commercialised” operations, although it has not specified what those services will ultimately look like or set a timetable for their roll-out.
Elsewhere, driverless ride-hailing services have already begun operating commercially.
Waymo operates autonomous taxi services in several cities in the United States, while Zoox, owned by Amazon, is testing purpose-built robotaxis in San Francisco and Las Vegas.
In mainland China, Apollo Go has deployed commercial robotaxi fleets in cities such as Shenzhen.

data.title: Foreign carmakers lose more traction in China as luxury sales slide
data.description:
International luxury car brands from Mercedes-Benz to Land Rover took a further beating in China last month as wealthy consumers continued to shun expensive petrol-powered vehicles.
Struggling against competition from China’s rising electric vehicle (EV) powerhouses, international marques would find it more difficult to retain their market share and maintain profitability in the world’s largest automotive market, analysts said.
According to data from the China Passenger Car Association (CPCA), luxury auto brands reported sales of 162,224 vehicles last month, down 29.5 per cent from the same period in 2025.
The luxury category includes only two indigenous EV brands, BYD’s Yangwang and JAC Group’s Maextro. Most of the more than 10 other marques are international brands, including BMW, Jaguar and Infiniti, that offer only petrol cars in the mainland market.
“The luxury segment was believed to be international brands’ stronghold, as more low-priced electric cars developed by Chinese companies showed an upper hand in the mass market,” said Zhao Zhen, a sales director at Shanghai dealer Wan Zhuo Auto. “The downward trend is set to continue amid consumers’ waning demand for premium cars and increasing penchant for EVs due to rising crude oil prices.”
CPCA data showed that luxury car deliveries during the first half slumped 17.9 per cent year on year to 967,929 units.
In the three months ending June, luxury cars accounted for 10.2 per cent of total new vehicle sales on the mainland, compared with 12 per cent in 2024 and 10.2 per cent last year, it added.
Two decades ago, established conventional carmakers such as Volkswagen and Toyota held more than 80 per cent of the Chinese car market, as vehicles assembled locally through joint ventures proved far superior to indigenous models. But they have been eclipsed by Chinese EV assemblers including BYD and Leapmotor since 2023 due to their slow transition into EVs.

From January to June, deliveries of EVs, comprising pure electric and plug-in hybrid cars, jumped 14 per cent year on year to 4.7 million units, representing 54 per cent of the country’s total, CPCA data showed.
Battered by worries about their job prospects and income, most well-to-do and middle-income consumers in China have been cautious on purchases of big-ticket items, said Eric Han, a senior ­manager at Shanghai consultancy Suolei.
At the end of June, global consultancy AlixPartners predicted overall sales of light vehicles – passenger cars and pickups – in China would slump 10 per cent year on year to 24.6 million units in 2026, owing to a shaky economy and softening government support.
UBS predicted in an earlier report that diminished demand for foreign-branded cars would lead to an excess capacity of 10 million vehicles in the future, though it did not provide a clear time frame.
